headline: Sarkozy and the mystery of the Libyan money: A look back at a legal marathon
subhead: The appeals trial over suspicions of Libyan funding for the 2007 presidential campaign began on March 16. The case is filled with a cast of shady figures.

G7 environment ministers omit climate from talks: 'If we start talking about it, there is no more G7'
subhead: France, which is hosting the meeting on Thursday and Friday in Paris, has had to make concessions to ensure the participation of the US.

headline: Boris Pistorius, the man preparing Germany for war
subhead: The country's most popular politician for the past three years, who started serving as defense minister under Olaf Scholz, is set to unveil a military strategy review on Wednesday aimed at making the Bundeswehr the leading conventional army in Europe. Despite receiving unprecedented financial resources, the institution has been slow to reform.

headline: Sealing the internet: How Putin wants to cut Russia off from the rest of the world
subhead: Controlling information has become a matter of survival for the Russian leader. Since 2019, he has sought to create a 'sovereign RuNet' under his control and cut off from the world. The technical implementation of this project has accelerated since the start of the war in Ukraine in 2022.

headline: 'I was so exhausted, I started hitting myself. They laughed': Four Cuban asylum seekers recount their ordeal at Europe's borders
subhead: The European Court of Human Rights is expected to rule in the coming weeks on the pushbacks experienced by four Cubans at the border between Lithuania and Belarus, against the backdrop of a hybrid war with Russia.

'Why am I alive?': The Yazidi genocide as told by one of its survivors
subhead: Samih Taha, now a refugee in France, is one of the few survivors of the Islamic State group massacre of his village in northern Iraq on August 15, 2014. Then 17 years old, he went into debt to buy back his mother and sisters, who had been forced into sexual slavery. He told Le Monde his story and the tragedy that befell his family.

headline: The Belgian PM's troubling ambivalence toward Russia
subhead: Bart De Wever faces criticism for his unclear stances on the level of support the European Union should provide to Ukraine. The Flemish nationalist has defended himself against accusations of playing into Moscow's hands, insisting that he only aims to push the 27 member states forward.

headline: 'Bookshops now serve as sentinels of resistance to the radical rightward shift of society'
subhead: Christian Thorel, a prominent figure in independent book selling, was among the first to sound the alarm about billionaire Vincent Bolloré's ideological grip on the publishing sector, starting with the acquisition of France's top publishing group Hachette, in 2023.
